factsSummary: The appellant challenged legislation that conferred judicial power on a non-judicial body. The Full Federal Court upheld the legislation. The High Court granted special leave to appeal.
legalAnalysis: The Court held that the functions were inherently judicial, being the adjudication of disputes between parties. The conferral of such power on a body outside Chapter III was therefore invalid. This clarifies the boundary of judicial power following a period of uncertainty.
whyItMatters: This decision significantly narrows the scope for Parliament to confer adjudicative functions on non-judicial bodies, affecting administrative tribunals nationwide.
scoreJustification: Landmark HCA decision that clarifies previously unsettled law on the boundary of judicial power under Chapter III.
classificationReasoning: Primary area is constitutional law (separation of powers). Secondary area is administrative law as it impacts tribunal jurisdiction.
